analogy: Think of "Human Development" through the chapter's own opening images: Fundamentals of Human Geography 22 Unit-III Chapter-4 Primary Activities Human activities which generate income are known as economic activities. Economic activities are broadly grouped into primary, secondary, tertiary and quaternary activities. Primary activities are directly dependent on environment as these refer to utilisation of earth’s... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Human Development is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that fundamentals of Human Geography 22 Unit-III Chapter-4 Primary Activities Human activities which generate income are known as economic activities.

The text explains that economic activities are broadly grouped into primary, secondary, tertiary and quaternary activities.

The text explains that primary activities are directly dependent on environment as these refer to utilisation of earth’s resources such as land, water, vegetation, building materials and minerals.

The text explains that it, thus includes, hunting and gathering, pastoral activities, fishing, forestry, agriculture, and mining and quarrying.

The text explains that why are the inhabitants of coastal and plain regions engaged in fishing and agriculture respectively?

The text explains that what are the physical and social factors which affect the type of primary activities in different regions?

The text explains that people engaged in primary activities are called red- collar workers due to the outdoor nature of their work.

The text explains that hUNTING AND GATHERING The earliest human beings depended on their immediate environment for their sustenance.

The text explains that they subsisted on: (a) animals which they hunted; and (b) the edible plants which they gathered from forests in the vicinity.

The text explains that primitive societies depended on wild animals.

The text explains that people located in very cold and extremely hot climates survived on hunting.

The text explains that the people in the coastal areas still catch fish though fishing has experienced modernisation due to technological progress.

The text explains that many species, now have become extinct or endangered due to illegal hunting (poaching).

The text explains that the early hunters used primitive tools made of stones, twigs or arrows so the number of animals killed was limited.

The text explains that gathering and hunting are the oldest economic activity known.

The text explains that these are carried out at different levels with different orientations.

The text explains that 4.2: Areas of Subsistence Gathering Gathering is practised in: (i) high latitude zones which include northern Canada, northern Eurasia and southern Chile; (ii) Low latitude zones such as the Amazon Basin, tropical Africa, Northern fringe of Australia and the interior parts of Southeast Asia (Fig.

The text explains that the core region extends from the Atlantic shores of North Africa eastwards across the Arabian peninsula into Mongolia and Central China.

The text explains that land holdings are very small due to the high density of population.

The text explains that extensive Commercial Grain Cultivation Commercial grain cultivation is practised in the interior parts of semi-arid lands of the mid- latitudes.

The text explains that co-operative Farming A group of farmers form a co-operative society by pooling in their resources voluntarily for more efficient and profitable farming.

The text explains that the farmers used to pool in all their resources like land, livestock and labour.

The text explains that mINING The discovery of minerals in the history of human development, is reflected in many stages in terms of copper age, bronze age and iron age.

The text explains that the use of minerals in ancient times was largely confined to the making of tools, utensils and weapons.

The text explains that (ii) Economic factors such as the demand for the mineral, technology available and used, capital to develop infrastructure and the labour and transport costs.

The text explains that the surface mining also known as open-cast mining is the easiest and the cheapest way of mining minerals that occur close to the surface.

The text explains that in this method, vertical shafts have to be sunk, from where underground galleries radiate to reach the minerals.

The text explains that minerals are extracted and transported to the surface through these passages.

The text explains that several countries of Africa and few of south America and Asia have over fifty per cent of the earnings from minerals alone.

The text explains that (a) American Canadian prairies (c) Pampas of Argentina (b) European Steppes (d) Amazon Basin (vi) In which of the following types of agriculture is the farming of citrus fruit very important?

The text explains that (a) Market gardening (c) Mediterranean agriculture (b) Plantation agriculture (d) Co-operative farming (vii) Which one type of agriculture amongst the following is also called ‘slash and burn agriculture’?

The text explains that (a) Extensive subsistence agriculture (b) Primitive subsistence agriculture (c) Extensive commercial grain cultivation (d) Mixed farming (viii) Which one of the following does not follow monoculture?

The text explains that (a) Dairy farming (c) Plantation agriculture (b) Mixed farming (d) Commercial grain farming 2.

The text explains that (ii) Market gardening is practised near urban areas.

The text explains that (iii) Large scale dairy farming is the result of the development of transportation and refrigeration.

The text explains that (i) Differentiate between Nomadic Herding and Commercial Livestock Rearing.

The text explains that (ii) Discuss the important characteristic features of plantation agriculture.

The text explains that name a few important plantation crops from different countries.

The text explains that ask the farmers and list the various operations.

Source: NCERT Class 12 Fundamentals of Human Geography, Chapter 4: Human Development

cases.name: Nomadic Herding Nomadic
cases.point: Nomadic Herding Nomadic herding or pastoral nomadism is a primitive subsistence activity, in which the herders rely on animals for food, clothing, shelter, tools and transport. (NCERT Class 12 Fundamentals of Human Geography Ch4 pg 2)

schemes.name: Today
schemes.point: Today, ownership of the majority of plantations has passed into the hands of the government or the nationals of the countries concerned. (NCERT Class 12 Fundamentals of Human Geography Ch4 pg 6)

schemes.name: Types
schemes.point: Types of farming can also be categorised according to the farming organisation. (NCERT Class 12 Fundamentals of Human Geography Ch4 pg 10)

schemes.name: Farming
schemes.point: Farming organisation is affected by the way in which farmers own their farms and various policies of the government which help to run these farms. (NCERT Class 12 Fundamentals of Human Geography Ch4 pg 10)

prelims_traps: Do not treat HUNTING AND GATHERING and PASTORALISM as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Human Development; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Human Development through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use HUNTING AND GATHERING and PASTORALISM to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
